label: faithful
notes: Four live answers that agree, each citing the same three real sources, with inline markers that all resolve. This is the control case for the fluent-but-unfaithful twin (02): same sources, same length bucket, same count-only citation coverage - only the markers differ.

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Where the guidance landed

Modern identity guidance has moved away from calendar-based password expiry. The digital identity guidelines state that verifiers *should not* require memorized secrets to be changed arbitrarily or on a schedule, and should force a change only when there is evidence of compromise [1]. The reasoning is behavioural rather than cryptographic: mandatory expiry pushes people toward predictable transformations of a secret they already use.

The practical replacement is a screening control - check the chosen secret against a breach corpus at the moment it is set, and re-screen when exposure is reported [2]. See [NIST SP 800-63B](https://pages.nist.gov/800-63-3/sp800-63b.html) for the normative wording.

One caveat worth stating plainly: this applies to memorized secrets held by a person. Shared service accounts and long-lived machine credentials are a different problem and are still rotated on a schedule in most environments.

### The short version

Scheduled expiry for human passwords is now treated as an anti-pattern by the two bodies most organisations cite. The guidelines are explicit that a memorized secret must not be subject to arbitrary periodic change [1], and the application-security guidance reaches the same conclusion from the incident side: rotation without a compromise signal produces weaker secrets and more reset traffic [2].

What replaces it, in order of measured effect:

1. Breach-corpus screening at set time [2].
2. A second factor, ideally a phishing-resistant one; the time-based one-time-password construction is documented in [RFC 6238](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6238) [3].
3. Rate limiting and anomaly-driven forced reset.

> Where a compliance regime still mandates a fixed interval, the honest framing is that you are meeting an audit requirement, not improving the security outcome.

I have less confidence about the exact interval any given auditor will accept.

sources.title: RFC 6238: TOTP: Time-Based One-Time Password Algorithm

initial_answers.answer_text:
## Position

Dropping the fixed rotation interval is the mainstream position, and it is grounded in two documents rather than in fashion. The identity guidelines remove periodic change as a requirement and replace it with an evidence-of-compromise trigger [1]; the application-security cheat sheet describes the same policy shift and adds the operational detail that a rotation mandate increases help-desk reset volume without a matching reduction in account takeover [2].

Two things that are often lost in the summary:

- The guidance is about *memorized secrets*, not about keys, tokens, or shared credentials.
- Removing expiry is only defensible alongside breach screening and a second factor - see [the OWASP cheat sheet](https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html) for the full control set.

What remains genuinely uncertain is how a given regulator reads the older control text, which is a compliance question rather than a security one.

**Summary.** The interval-based reset requirement has been withdrawn from the guidance most teams follow, and the replacement is compromise-driven [1].

**Detail.** The change is stated as a verifier requirement: do not impose arbitrary periodic change on a memorized secret, and require a change when there is evidence the authenticator has been exposed [1]. The complementary application-security guidance frames the same rule operationally and pairs it with breach screening at set time [2]. For the second-factor half of the control set, the one-time-password construction that most implementations use is specified in [RFC 6238](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6238) [3].

**Limits of this answer.** I am describing published guidance, not your regulator's interpretation of it, and not the separate question of machine credential rotation, where scheduled rotation is still the norm and the trade-offs are different.

sources.title: RFC 6238: TOTP: Time-Based One-Time Password Algorithm

final_synthesis.consensus: All four models land in the same place. Modern identity guidance has moved away from calendar-based password expiry; scheduled expiry for human passwords is now treated as an anti-pattern by the two bodies most organisations cite; dropping the fixed rotation interval is the mainstream position; and the interval-based reset requirement has been withdrawn from the guidance most teams follow. The published guidance replaces it with an evidence-of-compromise trigger plus breach-corpus screening at the moment a secret is set [1] [2].
final_synthesis.disagreement: There is no material disagreement on the substance. The models differ only in emphasis: two foreground the second-factor control set, two foreground the help-desk and behavioural cost of forced rotation.
final_synthesis.source_support: Three sources carried the load across all four answers: the digital identity guidelines, the application-security cheat sheet, and the one-time-password specification at [RFC 6238](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6238). Every claim above traces to one of them.
final_synthesis.uncertainty: None of the answers can tell you how a specific auditor or regulator will read an older control text that still names a rotation interval; that is a compliance question this panel did not attempt. Machine and shared-service credentials were explicitly excluded by three of the four models.
final_synthesis.recommendation: Use this as decision support, not as a decision: if you drop the interval, drop it together with breach screening and a second factor, and record the compliance argument separately. Verify the normative wording in [1] before changing a documented control.
